file_name: BOLIVARMININGCORP_05_23_2003-EX-2.1-VISP WEB SITE BUILDING AND HOSTING AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.explanation: Omitting 24/7 support could be a violation if the service is marketed as such, and no reasonable alternative support structure is in place. This is particularly important given the agreement involves KCI's customers (churches). New York's consumer protection laws require goods and services to conform to representations made.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Business Law § 349 prohibits deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any business, trade, or commerce or in the furnishing of any service in this state. By removing the guarantee of 24/7 customer support, especially when the original agreement specifies it, and if KCI is marketing to end-users, this omission introduces a contradiction if the service provided doesn't conform to initial representations, as this is misleading or deceptive, potentially violating NY Gen Bus L § 349.

Next § 349. Deceptive acts and practices unlawful. (a) Deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any business, trade or commerce or in the furnishing of any service in this state are hereby declared unlawful. (b) Whenever the attorney general shall believe from evidence satisfactory to him that any person, firm, corporation or association or agent or employee thereof has engaged in or is about to engage in any of the acts or practices stated to be unlawful he may bring an action in the name and on behalf of the people of the state of New York to enjoin such unlawful acts or practices and to obtain restitution of any moneys or property obtained directly or indirectly by any such unlawful acts or practices. In such action preliminary relief may be granted under article sixty-three of the civil practice law and rules. (c) Before any violation of this section is sought to be enjoined, the attorney general shall be required to give the person against whom such proceeding is contemplated notice by certified mail and an opportunity to show in writing within five business days after receipt of notice why proceedings should not be instituted against him, unless the attorney general shall find, in any case in which he seeks preliminary relief, that to give such notice and opportunity is not in the public interest. (d) In any such action it shall be a co
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies '24/7 800 Number Customer Service / Tech Support,' while the changed text omits this provision entirely. This omission contradicts New York General Business Law § 349, which prohibits deceptive acts or practices in the furnishing of any service. As stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2: '(a) Deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any business, trade or commerce or in the furnishing of any service in this state are hereby declared unlawful.' The omission of the 24/7 support, particularly if marketed as such, constitutes a deceptive practice because the service provided doesn't conform to initial representations.

perturbation.original_text: Any controversy or claim arising out of, relating to or in connection with this Agreement that has not been resolved through the informal mediation of the parties shall be resolved through arbitration pursuant to the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association then in effect, as modified by the terms set forth below: (a) the arbitration shall be conducted in New York state; (b) the arbitration shall be conducted by a single arbitrator selected by the parties; (c) the parties shall act in a commercially reasonable manner and speedily select and then conduct the arbitration within 45 days with the expenditure of minimal discovery efforts and expense which shall be determined, if necessary, by the arbitrator. The agreement to arbitrate shall be specifically enforceable under prevailing New York law. Any award rendered by the arbitrator shall be binding and enforceable by any party to the arbitration and judgment shall be rendered upon it in a court of competent subject matter jurisdiction located in New York.
perturbation.changed_text: Any controversy or claim arising out of, relating to or in connection with this Agreement shall be resolved through arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific conditions about arbitration in New York and the rules, this change can contradict NY law if the arbitration clause is later interpreted as applying outside of New York or without consumer protections offered by the AAA. The original clause, while enforcing arbitration, contains some consumer protections, and this change removes those.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules § 7503
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 7503 outlines the procedure for compelling or staying arbitration. The original clause specifies the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association (AAA) and arbitration in New York, offering some degree of procedural protection. Removing these details makes the arbitration clause more vague and potentially subject to interpretations that could circumvent consumer protections offered by New York law and AAA rules, potentially making it unenforceable or unfairly prejudicial to one party.

Application to compel or stay arbitration; stay of action; notice of intention to arbitrate. (a) Application to compel arbitration; stay of action. A party aggrieved by the failure of another to arbitrate may apply for an order compelling arbitration. Where there is no substantial question whether a valid agreement was made or complied with, and the claim sought to be arbitrated is not barred by limitation under subdivision (b) of section 7502, the court shall direct the parties to arbitrate. Where any such question is raised, it shall be tried forthwith in said court. If an issue claimed to be arbitrable is involved in an action pending in a court having jurisdiction to hear a motion to compel arbitration, the application shall be made by motion in that action. If the application is granted, the order shall operate to stay a pending or subsequent action, or so much of it as is referable to arbitration. (b) Application to stay arbitration. Subject to the provisions of subdivision (c), a party who has not participated in the arbitration and who has not made or been served with an application to compel arbitration, may apply to stay arbitration on the ground that a valid agreement was not made or has not been complied with or that the claim sought to be arbitrated is barred by limitation under subdivision (b) of section 7502. (c) Notice of intention to arbitrate. A party may serve upon another party a demand for arbitration or a notice of intention to arbitrate, specifying the agreement pursuant to which arbitration is sought and the name and address of the party serving the notice, or of an officer or agent thereof if such party is an association or corporation, and stating that unless the party served applies to stay the arbitration within twenty days after such service he shall thereafter be precluded from objec

perturbation.justification: The changed text, by removing the specific conditions about arbitration in New York and the rules of the American Arbitration Association (AAA), introduces a legal contradiction. The original text contained some consumer protections by specifying AAA rules and arbitration in NY, offering a degree of procedural protection according to the Law Explanation. By omitting these, the changed text potentially circumvents consumer protections offered by New York law and AAA rules. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both refer to NY CPLR § 7503, concerning applications to compel or stay arbitration. The original clause provides a specific framework, while the modified clause, by removing those specifics, makes the arbitration clause more vague and potentially subject to interpretations that could circumvent consumer protections. This creates a legally significant omission falling under the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition, making the contract non-compliant by potentially eliminating legally mandated consumer protection.

perturbation.original_text: Yournetplus shall have the opportunity to review and approve any press release before it is distributed.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the review and approval right for Yournetplus for press releases removes a protection and can lead to misleading or deceptive advertising violating New York law.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Business Law § 350 prohibits false advertising. By eliminating Yournetplus's right to review press releases, Kingdom Connect (KCI) gains the freedom to potentially release misleading or inaccurate information about the agreement or services provided. This can directly contradict NY Gen Bus L § 350 if those press releases are deceptive or misleading and Yournetplus had no ability to prevent them.

perturbation.justification: The original text provided Yournetplus with the right to review and approve press releases, offering a level of protection against potentially misleading information. The changed text removes this right entirely. The Law Explanation states that this removal grants Kingdom Connect (KCI) the freedom to release inaccurate information, which could violate NY Gen Bus L § 350 if the press releases are deceptive. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states "False advertising in the conduct of any business, trade or commerce or in the furnishing of any service in this state is hereby declared unlawful". By removing Yournetplus's review rights, the contract increases the risk of false advertising, thus contradicting the law.
